fuzzy_prd: We need a translation preparation toolkit for our content management system. When editors write rich text — paragraphs, headings, lists, links, and formatting — we need to pull out just the translatable pieces so translators never see HTML infrastructure or link URLs they could accidentally break. The key steps are: first, escape any special characters in plain text so they're safe to embed in markup; second, strip technical attributes off inline tags and save them in a side map so translators only touch clean markup; third, after translation comes back, check the result still has no stray attributes (except the identifiers we put in); fourth, render a translated piece back to plain text or restore it to full HTML by plugging the saved attributes back in by their identifiers (not by position). We also need to handle full document extraction — pulling out each paragraph, heading, or list item as a separate segment — and later put translated segments back into the original document structure. Handle the identifier tag numbering the same way as the attribute-lifting step does (a-tags get a1, a2, etc. in document order). Finally, we need link-integrity checks: after translation, no new link identifiers should appear that weren't in the source.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def normalize_source(html: str) -> Tuple[str, Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]]

trigger_keywords: what tags are allowed in inline strings
what is a disallowed tag error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Allowed inline tags: a, abbr, acronym, b, code, em, i, strong, br. Block-level tags (p, h1, ul, li, img, etc.) are not permitted inside an inline string. Encountering a disallowed tag emits 'error=disallowed_tag\ntag=<tagname>'.

Identifiers are generated per tag name in document order: the first anchor becomes 'a1', the second 'a2', etc. Each tag name has its own counter independently. An anchor with no attributes gets no generated id. Output: 'string=<normalized>' then 'attrs=<json_object>'.

trigger_keywords: what attribute is allowed in translated fragments
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In translated fragments (validate_translated), NO attributes are allowed except an 'id' attribute on an 'a' tag. Any other attribute triggers 'error=disallowed_attributes'. A block-level tag triggers 'error=disallowed_tag' with 'tag=<name>'.

trigger_keywords: how are segments extracted from a document
what is trimmed at segment boundaries
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
During extract_segments: (1) A
at the very start or end of a segment is excluded from it. (2) An inline tag wrapping an entire segment is unwrapped so only its contents form the segment. (3) Empty inline tags contribute nothing. (4) Surrounding/internal insignificant whitespace at segment edges is trimmed. Output: 'count=' then 'segment[i]=' and 'attrs[i]=' for each.

trigger_keywords: is restoration by position or identifier
order independence of id attribute lookup
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attribute restoration (to_html / restore_segments) is strictly by identifier, NOT by position. Each tag's 'id' attribute is looked up in the provided attrs map and replaced by the corresponding attributes. Identifiers may appear in any order relative to the map.

trigger_keywords: how are foreign link identifiers reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: check_translation_links: When translation contains identifiers not in source, emits 'error=unrecognised_ids\nids=<sorted comma-and-space separated list>'. When all identifiers are recognized, emits 'foreign_ids=' (with nothing after the =).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Handle the identifier tag numbering the same way as the attribute-lifting step does
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The normalize_source operation in Feature 2 — generates per-tag-name counters (a1, a2... for anchors) in document order, stored in a side map keyed by those identifiers

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md specification and the golden reference implementation for details.

feature1_plaintext_to_string.json.description: Convert a raw plaintext value into a normalized inline-HTML string. Every HTML special character in the text is escaped, embedded newlines are turned into self-closing line-break tags, and non-ASCII characters are preserved verbatim. The output is the resulting inline-HTML string.

feature2_normalize_source_html.json.description: Normalize an inline-HTML fragment coming from authored content by stripping every tag attribute out into a side map keyed by a generated per-tag identifier, leaving only the translatable markup. Tags are numbered per tag name in document order (a1, a2, ... for the first, second anchor, and so on) and the stripped attributes are returned keyed by that identifier. Only standard inline tags are allowed; a block-level or non-inline tag is rejected with a neutral disallowed-tag error naming the offending tag.

input.html: Bread is a staple food prepared from a dough

cases.expected_output: string=Bread is a staple food prepared from a dough attrs={"a1": {"href": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Staple_food"}, "a2": {"href": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dough"}}

feature3_validate_translated_html.json.description: Validate an inline-HTML fragment that has already been translated. Translated fragments carry no attributes (these were stripped before translation) except that anchors may carry an identifier. The fragment is accepted and echoed back when it contains only standard inline tags; a block-level tag is rejected with a disallowed-tag error and any tag carrying a real attribute (other than an id on an anchor) is rejected with a disallowed-attributes error.

feature4_string_to_text.json.description: Render an inline-HTML string down to its plain visible text. All inline tags are removed keeping their textual content, line-break tags become newline characters, and HTML entities are decoded to the characters they represent. The output is the resulting plain text.

input.data: This is a paragraph. This is some bold and now italic text

cases.expected_output: This is a paragraph. This is some bold and now italic text

feature5_string_to_html.json.description: Render an inline-HTML string back to full HTML by restoring previously stripped attributes. The string contains placeholder tags carrying an identifier; a supplied map associates each identifier with the original set of attributes. Each placeholder identifier is replaced by its mapped attributes, so the association is by identifier and not by position. The output is the reconstructed HTML.

input.data: Bread is a dough prepared from a staple food of flour and water

cases.expected_output: Bread is a dough prepared from a staple food of flour and water

feature6_extract_segments.json.description: Extract the ordered list of translatable segments from a rich-text document. Each contiguous run of visible text together with the inline tags wrapping it becomes one segment; block-level structure is not part of a segment. Leading/trailing line-break tags inside a segment are excluded, an inline tag containing block content is split, and empty inline tags contribute nothing. Each extracted segment is itself a normalized inline string (attributes stripped to a per-segment map). The output reports how many segments were found and, for each, the normalized segment markup and its attribute map in order.

input.html:
Foo bar baz

This is a paragraph. This is some bold and now italic text

<script> this should be interpreted as text.

  • List item one
  • List item two

feature7_restore_segments.json.description: Restore translated segments back into a document template. The template contains placeholder tags each carrying a zero-based position; each placeholder is replaced by the rendered HTML of the segment at that position, with that segment's stripped attributes restored. The output is the reconstructed document.

feature8_1_list_link_ids.json.description: Collect the set of anchor identifiers present in an inline-HTML fragment. Only anchor tags carrying an identifier contribute, each identifier is counted once regardless of how many times it appears, and the output lists the unique identifiers in sorted order (empty when there are none).

feature8_2_check_translation_links.json.description: Validate that a translated fragment introduces no anchor identifiers that were not present in its source fragment. When every anchor identifier in the translation also exists in the source the check passes and reports no foreign identifiers. When the translation contains identifiers absent from the source the check fails with a neutral unrecognised-ids error listing the offending identifiers in sorted order.

feature9_segment_path.json.description: Manipulate the dotted path that addresses a translatable segment within a content tree. Wrapping prepends a new leading component to the path; unwrapping pops and returns the first component together with the remaining path; setting the order assigns an ordinal to the segment without changing its path. The output reports the resulting path components.
